transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Alex. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, thanks for having me. I guess... I don't know, maybe just how things have been going lately? It feels like I keep messing up, like I can't even get the little things right. But, I mean, it's probably not that big of a deal, right? Just a rough patch, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling a bit overwhelmed and frustrated with how things have been going for you lately. You mentioned feeling like you're messing up, even with the little things. That can be really tough. Can you share more about what specific situations or moments have been bothering you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I guess. I mean, there was this one time I overslept and missed my alarm for work. It just set off this whole chain reaction, you know? I thought, "Great, now my day is ruined." I ended up calling in sick and... well, I didn't handle it well. Just felt like everything spiraled from there. But, I don't know, maybe it was just a one-off thing? It happens, right?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like missing your alarm really threw you off and led to a lot of frustration. You mentioned feeling like your day was ruined and that it spiraled from there. It’s understandable to feel that way when something unexpected happens. Do you often find that one small setback can lead to bigger feelings or reactions for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I guess so. It’s like, one little thing goes wrong, and suddenly it feels like everything's falling apart. I don't know why I react that way. I mean, I try to stay calm, but it just builds up, and then I end up feeling anxious and angry. It’s frustrating because I know it’s not the end of the world, but in the moment, it feels huge. I just wish I could handle it better, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re experiencing a lot of pressure when things don’t go as planned, and that can lead to feelings of anxiety and anger. You recognize that it’s not the end of the world, yet in those moments, it feels overwhelming. That frustration with your reaction is understandable. Can you think of any patterns or beliefs you might have about how things should go or how you should handle situations when they don’t?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hmm, I guess I have this idea that I should be able to manage everything without a hitch. Like, if I can't even wake up on time, what does that say about me? It feels like I should have it all together, and when I don’t, it just... I don’t know, it makes me feel like a failure. I mean, I know everyone has off days, but I can’t help but think I should be better at this. It’s like I put this pressure on myself to be perfect, and when I slip up, it just feels like a big deal.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you hold yourself to a really high standard, believing that you should manage everything perfectly. That pressure to be perfect can be really heavy, and when you don’t meet those expectations, it leads to feelings of failure and disappointment. It’s understandable to feel that way, especially when you care about doing well. Can you recall where that belief about needing to be perfect might have come from?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m not really sure. I mean, I guess growing up, there was always this emphasis on doing well in school and being responsible. It was like, if you didn’t get good grades or if you messed up, it was a big deal. I think I just internalized that, you know? Like, if I’m not doing everything right, then I’m not good enough. But I don’t really want to think about it too much. It’s just how I’ve always been, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the emphasis on doing well and being responsible in your upbringing has really shaped how you view yourself and your performance. Internalizing that pressure can create a strong belief that your worth is tied to how well you do things. It makes sense that you might want to avoid digging too deep into those feelings, especially if they feel heavy. How do you feel when you think about that connection between your past experiences and your current feelings of pressure?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I don’t know, it feels a bit uncomfortable to think about. Like, I get it, but it’s hard to face. I mean, I don’t want to blame anyone or anything, but it’s just... it’s a lot to carry. Sometimes I wish I could just let it go and not feel so weighed down by it all. But then I think, if I don’t hold myself to those standards, what does that mean for me? It’s confusing, I guess. I just want to figure it out without feeling so overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel uncomfortable when reflecting on those deeper connections. Carrying that weight can be exhausting, and the idea of letting go of those standards can feel both freeing and frightening at the same time. You’re caught in a tension between wanting to release that pressure and fearing what it might mean for your sense of self. It’s a lot to navigate. What do you think might help you explore this tension without feeling so overwhelmed?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m not really sure. Maybe just talking about it more? I mean, it helps to get it out, but I still feel hesitant. I guess I could try to take small steps, like maybe not being so hard on myself when things don’t go perfectly. But it’s tough to change how I think overnight. I don’t want to feel like I’m just making excuses for myself, either. I guess I just need to find a way to balance it all without feeling like I’m losing control.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re open to exploring this further, and that’s a great step. Talking about it can definitely help, and it makes sense that you want to approach it gradually. Finding that balance between self-compassion and accountability is important, and it’s okay to take your time with it. You mentioned not wanting to feel like you’re making excuses—what does that idea of “making excuses” mean to you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it means not taking responsibility for my actions or just brushing things off when I should be doing better. Like, if I start to let myself off the hook too easily, I worry I’ll just end up slacking off or not trying hard enough. It’s like I want to be able to acknowledge when I mess up, but I also don’t want to beat myself up over it. It’s a fine line, I guess. I just don’t want to feel like I’m not living up to my own expectations.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re highlighting a really important struggle—the desire to take responsibility while also wanting to be kind to yourself. It sounds like you’re aware of the potential for self-compassion to feel like it might lead to slacking off, which creates a lot of pressure. It’s definitely a fine line to walk. How do you think you might start to recognize when you’re being too hard on yourself versus when you’re holding yourself accountable in a healthy way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe I could start by paying attention to how I feel after I react to something. Like, if I mess up and immediately start feeling anxious or angry, that might be a sign that I’m being too hard on myself. I could try to take a step back and ask if I’m being fair to myself in those moments. Maybe even writing it down could help, like keeping track of my thoughts and feelings when things don’t go as planned. That way, I could see patterns and maybe figure out when I’m being too critical versus when I’m just trying to hold myself accountable. It’s a start, at least.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach! Paying attention to your feelings after reacting can provide valuable insights into how you’re treating yourself in those moments. Writing down your thoughts and feelings could also help you identify patterns and clarify when you’re being overly critical versus when you’re holding yourself accountable in a constructive way. It’s a great starting point for building that balance you’re looking for. How do you feel about implementing this idea?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit hopeful about it, actually. It sounds manageable, and having a way to track my thoughts might help me see things more clearly. I mean, I can be pretty hard on myself, so maybe writing it down will help me recognize when I’m crossing that line. I just hope I can stick with it and not get overwhelmed. But it’s nice to think that I might be able to change how I react to things, even a little bit.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling hopeful about this approach! Having a manageable way to track your thoughts can definitely help bring clarity and awareness. It’s completely normal to have concerns about sticking with it, but starting small and being gentle with yourself along the way can make a big difference. Remember, it’s about progress, not perfection. What do you think might help you stay motivated to keep this practice going?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe setting small goals for myself could help, like just writing a few times a week instead of trying to do it every day. That way, it doesn’t feel so overwhelming. I could also remind myself why I want to do this in the first place—like, to feel less stressed and more in control. Maybe sharing my progress with someone I trust could keep me accountable, too. I just want to make sure I don’t let it slip away like I have with other things before.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like great strategies! Setting small, achievable goals can definitely make the process feel less daunting, and reminding yourself of your motivation is a powerful way to stay focused. Sharing your progress with someone you trust can also provide support and accountability, which can be really helpful. It’s understandable to want to avoid letting this slip away like before. How do you feel about the idea of checking in with yourself regularly to see how you’re doing with these goals?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think checking in with myself regularly could be really beneficial. It might help me stay on track and see how I’m feeling about everything. I could set aside a little time each week to reflect on how it’s going and adjust things if I need to. I guess it’s just about creating a routine that feels right for me. I’m a bit nervous about it, but I think it could help me stay accountable without feeling too much pressure. It’s nice to think I might actually be able to make some changes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re developing a thoughtful plan that feels manageable for you. Setting aside time each week to reflect and adjust as needed can create a routine that supports your growth without adding too much pressure. It’s completely normal to feel nervous about making changes, but it’s encouraging to hear that you’re feeling hopeful about the possibility of progress. What’s one small step you feel ready to take this week to start this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could start by just writing down my thoughts after a stressful day or when something doesn’t go as planned. Just a few sentences to capture how I felt and what happened. That feels doable. I can try to do it a couple of times this week and see how it goes. It’s a small step, but hopefully, it’ll help me get into the habit of checking in with myself. I’m a bit nervous, but I think it’s a good start.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a perfect first step! Writing down your thoughts after a stressful day or a challenging moment can be a great way to start building that habit of self-reflection. It’s manageable, and it allows you to check in with yourself without feeling overwhelmed. It’s completely normal to feel a bit nervous, but it’s encouraging to see you taking this step. How do you feel about the idea of celebrating those small moments of reflection, no matter how they go?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think celebrating those small moments could be really helpful. It might remind me that even just taking the time to reflect is a step in the right direction, regardless of what I write down. I guess it’s about recognizing that I’m trying, and that’s something to acknowledge. Maybe I could treat myself to something small after I do it, like a favorite snack or just some time to relax. It’s nice to think that I could find a way to appreciate my efforts, even if they’re small.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a wonderful idea! Celebrating those small moments can reinforce your efforts and remind you that every step counts. Treating yourself to something you enjoy, like a favorite snack or some relaxation time, can create a positive association with the practice of reflection. It’s great to hear that you’re focusing on appreciating your efforts, no matter how small they may seem. How do you feel about starting this practice and incorporating those celebrations into your routine?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel pretty good about it, actually. It seems like a nice way to make the process more enjoyable and less daunting. I like the idea of rewarding myself for just trying, even if it’s just a little thing. It makes it feel more like a positive experience rather than another thing to stress about. I’m hopeful that this will help me build a better relationship with myself and how I handle things. I’m ready to give it a shot and see how it goes!
